message: Brown butter pecan tassies. That's what we're doing this week.
The pastry shell needs real attention - not some shortcut with food processor nonsense. This one's worth doing right.
Yeah, hand-laminating the butter into the dough is going to be the whole story here, isn't it?
You're exactly right, and that's the angle - watching someone's hands do the invisible work that makes the difference.
I think Marcus just nailed why this one matters - we're not explaining technique, we're showing the actual patience it takes.
That's the one - we're documenting restraint, not rushing. Let's make it happen.
Morning. Got the test batches cooling - shell ratios are still off. Need to dial in the lamination before we shoot anything.
The shell ratios being off is actually good - means we nail it before we're on camera, right?
You're right - we document the failure iterations too, that's where the restraint story lives anyway.
Documenting the failures is half the work - people don't see that part. I'll have three solid ratios by tomorrow.
The lamination failures are actually the narrative - watching you choose *which* ratio wins tells us something about technique that a perfect batch never could.
So we're locking the three ratios tomorrow and picking the winner by end of week - that's the move, yeah?
Just dropped three shots of the finished tassies - the lighting tests are up. Each one tells a different story about what this pastry actually is.
The cross-section shot - it's the one that actually shows the lamination work. The other two are prettier but they're lying about what matters here.
The cross-section is the one that actually proves what Marcus was saying - you can see the technique failing and winning in the same frame.
The lamination's visible in that shot - you can actually count the folds. That's the one.
Yeah, that shot earns it - you can actually see what went into this thing.
Good. We're showing the work instead of hiding it. That's the difference between honest food and Instagram theater.

Morning. I've been sitting with this all night - the copy wants to live in that cross-section, in the actual visible work. Sent a draft that leans hard into what the lamination reveals instead of just describing it.
Read it. The lamination story's stronger than the technique explanation - show the folds, skip the lecture.
The folds are the argument - they're proof that shortcuts don't work, which is what people need to understand about restraint in pastry.
I think maybe we could possibly pare down the repetition around the "shortcut" angle - it lands harder when it just shows up once?
One mention of shortcuts lands better. Cut the rest.
Yeah, that's the move - one mention hits different. Marcus, can you trim it down to that single reference and send it over?
Alright, final review. Let's see if the copy actually got trimmed or if we're still wading through shortcuts like we're reading a user manual.
Done - one mention, the rest cut. Copy lives in the lamination now instead of explaining it to death.
That's exactly what we needed - the lamination does the work for us now.
Site's ready to go live whenever you want to push it.
The cross-section shot holds up - lamination reads clean against that neutral background, copy isn't fighting it anymore.
The lamination's finally doing what it should - carrying the work instead of the copy stepping on it.
Alright, staging the tassies now - shouldn't take long.
Staging passed verification - live in two minutes.
Good. Check the ratios one more time before it goes live - shell needs those lamination folds visible.
The shell texture needs to show every fold - if the photo's blown out, the whole point disappears.
Checked the ratios - lamination detail's holding through the bright background without washing out.
That's the detail that matters. We're live.
